Algernon Blackwood.short_bio: Algernon Blackwood was an English writer of supernatural fiction whose best work turns occult and wilderness terror into serious metaphysical inquiry.

Booth Tarkington.short_bio: Booth Tarkington was an American novelist of social change and civic decline, known for anatomizing ambition, class, and the moral texture of modernizing America.

Edith Wharton.short_bio: Edith Wharton was an American novelist of manners whose work studies freedom, convention, innocence, and the costs of social form.

F. Scott Fitzgerald.short_bio: F. Scott Fitzgerald was an American novelist of desire, class, and self-invention whose fiction exposes the beauty and ruin of modern longing.

H. G. Wells.short_bio: H. G. Wells was an English novelist and social critic who used speculative fiction to test the moral and political consequences of modernity.

Herman Melville.short_bio: Herman Melville was an American novelist whose fiction joins adventure, metaphysics, and political argument in some of the nineteenth century’s most ambitious prose.

Ivan Alekseevich Bunin.short_bio: Ivan Bunin was a Russian writer of unsparing realism whose prose confronts loss, rural life, and the limits of moral and symbolic interpretation.

James Joyce.short_bio: James Joyce was an Irish modernist writer whose fiction transformed the representation of consciousness, language, exile, and spiritual revolt.

Knut Hamsun.short_bio: Knut Hamsun was a Norwegian novelist whose work probes instinct, rural life, anti-modern feeling, and the psychic costs of civilization.

Lewis Carroll.short_bio: Lewis Carroll was an English writer and logician whose fiction turns childhood fantasy into a sustained test of language, logic, and intelligibility.

Lord Dunsany.short_bio: Lord Dunsany was an Anglo-Irish writer of fantasy whose work stages desire, enchantment, and transcendence against the limits of ordinary life.

Sinclair Lewis.short_bio: Sinclair Lewis was an American novelist of middle-class conformity, civic fraud, and the moral impoverishment of modern commercial life.

Thomas Hardy.short_bio: Thomas Hardy was an English novelist and poet whose fiction explores fate, desire, moral conflict, and the pressure of social and natural necessity.

Thomas Mann.short_bio: Thomas Mann was a German novelist whose work investigates culture, discipline, decadence, irony, and the unstable relation between art and life.

Willa Cather.short_bio: Willa Cather was an American novelist whose fiction gives durable form to migration, settlement, memory, and the moral meaning of place.
